properties.name: Ada Lovelace
properties.summary: Mathematician and writer whose 1843 translation and Notes explored the possibilities of Babbage's Analytical Engine.

anchor_records.display_name: Ada Lovelace

properties.name: Ada Lovelace
properties.summary: English mathematician and writer associated with the Analytical Engine.

properties.summary: Babbage's unrealized programmable general-purpose mechanical computing design.

properties.summary: A number sequence used in Lovelace's Note G procedure.

anchor_records.display_name: Scientific Memoirs, volume 3

properties.name: Scientific Memoirs, volume 3
properties.summary: The 1843 English publication containing Menabrea's paper, Lovelace's translation, and Notes A-G.

anchor_records.display_name: Ada was born in London in 1815, long before electronic computers existed.

properties.fact_boundary: Biographical fact; do not invent childhood memories.
properties.kid_summary: Ada was born in London in 1815, long before electronic computers existed.
properties.text: Augusta Ada Byron, later Countess of Lovelace, was born in London on 10 December 1815.

anchor_records.display_name: Ada's parents were Annabella Milbanke and the poet Lord Byron.

properties.fact_boundary: Family relationship only; avoid diagnosing motives or private feelings.
properties.kid_summary: Ada's parents were Annabella Milbanke and the poet Lord Byron.
properties.text: Ada was the daughter of Annabella Milbanke and the poet Lord Byron.

anchor_records.display_name: Ada studied serious mathematics at a time when girls rarely received that opportunity.

properties.fact_boundary: Describe the historical access barrier without claiming Ada was the only educated woman.
properties.kid_summary: Ada studied serious mathematics at a time when girls rarely received that opportunity.
properties.text: Ada received private mathematical education that was unusual for a woman in early nineteenth-century Britain.

anchor_records.display_name: Ada learned with help from accomplished thinkers including Mary Somerville and Augustus De Morgan.

properties.fact_boundary: Do not collapse different teaching relationships into one classroom scene.
properties.kid_summary: Ada learned with help from accomplished thinkers including Mary Somerville and Augustus De Morgan.
properties.text: Mary Somerville and Augustus De Morgan were important figures in Ada's mathematical education.

anchor_records.display_name: Ada met Charles Babbage in 1833 through Mary Somerville.

properties.fact_boundary: The exact dialogue and room details are not established by this claim.
properties.kid_summary: Ada met Charles Babbage in 1833 through Mary Somerville.
properties.text: Mary Somerville introduced Ada to Charles Babbage in June 1833.

anchor_records.display_name: Ada saw part of Babbage's Difference Engine working and became fascinated by it.

properties.fact_boundary: A demonstration is supported; sensory details and spoken reactions require reconstruction labels.
properties.kid_summary: Ada saw part of Babbage's Difference Engine working and became fascinated by it.
properties.text: Babbage demonstrated a small working section of the Difference Engine to Ada.

anchor_records.display_name: Ada and Babbage kept exchanging ideas about mathematics and machines for many years.

properties.fact_boundary: Do not romanticize the relationship or invent private conversations.
properties.kid_summary: Ada and Babbage kept exchanging ideas about mathematics and machines for many years.
properties.text: Lovelace and Babbage maintained a long intellectual friendship centered on mathematics and his engine designs.

anchor_records.display_name: Ada translated an important French paper about the Analytical Engine into English.

properties.fact_boundary: Credit Menabrea as the original author and Lovelace as translator and note author.
properties.kid_summary: Ada translated an important French paper about the Analytical Engine into English.
properties.text: In 1843 Lovelace published an English translation of Luigi Menabrea's French account of the Analytical Engine.

anchor_records.display_name: Ada spent months developing the translation and her own detailed notes.

properties.fact_boundary: Do not invent a daily schedule or exact desk scene.
properties.kid_summary: Ada spent months developing the translation and her own detailed notes.
properties.text: Lovelace developed her translation and notes over roughly nine months in 1842 and 1843 while discussing the work with Babbage.

anchor_records.display_name: Ada's added notes were much longer than the paper she translated.

properties.fact_boundary: Approximate comparative length, not a page-count claim across every edition.
properties.kid_summary: Ada's added notes were much longer than the paper she translated.
properties.text: Lovelace's Notes A-G were approximately three times as long as Menabrea's original paper.

anchor_records.display_name: Ada's published notes were signed with her initials, A.A.L.

properties.fact_boundary: Do not imply the initials made her authorship secret to every contemporary reader.
properties.kid_summary: Ada's published notes were signed with her initials, A.A.L.
properties.text: The 1843 publication credited Lovelace's notes with the initials A.A.L.

anchor_records.display_name: Ada's publication became the major detailed English explanation of the Analytical Engine available while Babbage was alive.

properties.fact_boundary: Use the bounded museum formulation; do not claim it was the only mention of the engine in English.
properties.kid_summary: Ada's publication became the major detailed English explanation of the Analytical Engine available while Babbage was alive.
properties.text: Her translation and notes formed the only extensive English publication about the Analytical Engine during Babbage's lifetime.

anchor_records.display_name: Ada explained that the Difference Engine followed a narrower job, while the Analytical Engine was designed for many kinds of procedures.

properties.fact_boundary: Keep the distinction conceptual; avoid mapping every modern computer feature directly onto the designs.
properties.kid_summary: Ada explained that the Difference Engine followed a narrower job, while the Analytical Engine was designed for many kinds of procedures.
properties.text: Lovelace emphasized that the specialized Difference Engine and the more general Analytical Engine worked on fundamentally different principles.

anchor_records.display_name: The Analytical Engine was planned to follow different sets of instructions, not just repeat one calculation.

properties.fact_boundary: It was a design, not a completed modern computer.
properties.kid_summary: The Analytical Engine was planned to follow different sets of instructions, not just repeat one calculation.
properties.text: The Analytical Engine was conceived as a programmable general-purpose mechanical computing design rather than a machine for one fixed calculation.

anchor_records.display_name: The design separated stored values from the part doing operations and used punched cards for instructions.

properties.fact_boundary: Use historical terms first; modern analogies are explanatory, not claims of identical hardware.
properties.kid_summary: The design separated stored values from the part doing operations and used punched cards for instructions.
properties.text: Babbage's Analytical Engine plans separated a store for values from a mill for operations and used punched cards to control work.

anchor_records.display_name: Ada realized a programmable machine might work with symbols and patterns, not only arithmetic answers.

properties.fact_boundary: This is a conceptual possibility in her notes, not evidence that the machine actually processed text.
properties.kid_summary: Ada realized a programmable machine might work with symbols and patterns, not only arithmetic answers.
properties.text: Lovelace reasoned that if relationships could be represented in the engine's operations, it might manipulate symbols or other quantities as well as ordinary numbers.

anchor_records.display_name: Ada imagined that encoded musical relationships might let such a machine create music.

properties.fact_boundary: A speculative possibility in the 1843 Notes; no music was produced by the unbuilt engine.
properties.kid_summary: Ada imagined that encoded musical relationships might let such a machine create music.
properties.text: Lovelace suggested that a sufficiently represented system of musical relationships might allow the engine to compose elaborate music.

anchor_records.display_name: In Note G, Ada laid out a detailed procedure for calculating Bernoulli numbers with the Engine.

properties.fact_boundary: Describe the published procedure precisely without pretending it ran on a completed engine.
properties.kid_summary: In Note G, Ada laid out a detailed procedure for calculating Bernoulli numbers with the Engine.
properties.text: Lovelace's Note G included a detailed table of steps for the Analytical Engine to calculate Bernoulli numbers.

anchor_records.display_name: Ada is often called the first computer programmer because of Note G, but careful history explains why that label is debated.

properties.fact_boundary: Always preserve the qualification; do not present a contested title as uncontested fact.
properties.kid_summary: Ada is often called the first computer programmer because of Note G, but careful history explains why that label is debated.
properties.text: The Note G procedure is often described as the first published computer program or algorithm, although historians debate simplified 'first programmer' labels.

anchor_records.display_name: The full Analytical Engine was never built in Ada and Babbage's lifetimes.

properties.fact_boundary: Do not describe runtime results as observed historical events.
properties.kid_summary: The full Analytical Engine was never built in Ada and Babbage's lifetimes.
properties.text: Babbage never completed the Analytical Engine; only plans and trial pieces were produced.

anchor_records.display_name: A machine demonstration

properties.imagination_note: The demonstration is historical; the room, movements, and inner reaction are reconstructed.
properties.scene: Imagine brass wheels turning while a guide inspired by Ada leans closer to follow how one motion produces the next.
properties.title: A machine demonstration

anchor_records.display_name: Notes beside a translation

properties.imagination_note: The translation and months of work are historical; this exact desk moment is invented.
properties.scene: Pages of French and English sit beside diagrams while the guide checks how each explanation connects to the Engine's design.
properties.title: Notes beside a translation

anchor_records.display_name: Instructions as cards

properties.imagination_note: Punched-card control belongs to the design; this teaching demonstration is invented.
properties.scene: The guide arranges pretend cards into a sequence and explains that changing instructions could change the work performed.
properties.title: Instructions as cards

anchor_records.display_name: Patterns that might become music

properties.imagination_note: Lovelace proposed musical possibilities; the room and activity are reconstruction.
properties.scene: A page of musical notes sits beside numerical patterns as the guide wonders how relationships could be encoded for a machine.
properties.title: Patterns that might become music

anchor_records.display_name: Following Note G

properties.imagination_note: The published table is historical; the live explanation and gestures are invented.
properties.scene: The guide traces a table one operation at a time, checking which value must move or change next.
properties.title: Following Note G

anchor_records.display_name: A careful museum label

properties.imagination_note: The attribution debate is real; this particular museum conversation is invented.
properties.scene: The guide compares two labels: one calls Ada the first programmer, while the other explains the evidence and the historical debate.
properties.title: A careful museum label

anchor_records.display_name: How was the Analytical Engine different from the Difference Engine?

properties.answer: The Difference Engine was designed for a narrower family of calculations. The Analytical Engine was planned to follow changing instructions for more general procedures.
properties.hint: Think about one fixed job versus many instruction sets.
properties.prompt: How was the Analytical Engine different from the Difference Engine?

anchor_records.display_name: What did Ada add to the Engine project?

properties.answer: She translated Menabrea's account, added much longer explanatory notes, described wider possibilities, and published a detailed Bernoulli-number procedure.
properties.hint: Look at the translation, Notes A-G, and Note G.
properties.prompt: What did Ada add to the Engine project?

anchor_records.display_name: Did Ada think computers could make music?

properties.answer: She proposed that an engine might compose music if musical relationships could be represented for its operations. The unbuilt Engine never actually produced music.
properties.hint: Separate a proposed possibility from a completed demonstration.
properties.prompt: Did Ada think computers could make music?

anchor_records.display_name: Tell me about Ada seeing Babbage's machine.

properties.answer: Use the machine-demonstration reconstruction, then ground it in the supported meeting and demonstration claims.
properties.prompt: Tell me about Ada seeing Babbage's machine.

anchor_records.display_name: Tell me a story about Ada writing her notes.

properties.answer: Use the translation-desk reconstruction and distinguish the documented nine-month work from invented moment-by-moment detail.
properties.hint: The work is documented; the exact desk scene is not.
properties.prompt: Tell me a story about Ada writing her notes.

anchor_records.display_name: Tell me a story about the Bernoulli-number plan.

properties.answer: Use the Bernoulli-table reconstruction and explain that the published procedure was designed for a machine that was not completed.
properties.hint: A written procedure is not the same as a successful historical run.
properties.prompt: Tell me a story about the Bernoulli-number plan.

anchor_records.display_name: Who helped Ada study mathematics?

properties.answer: Mary Somerville and Augustus De Morgan were important figures in her mathematical education.
properties.hint: One introduced Ada to Babbage; the other taught through correspondence.

anchor_records.display_name: What number sequence appeared in Ada's Note G procedure?

properties.prompt: What number sequence appeared in Ada's Note G procedure?

anchor_records.display_name: Was the complete Analytical Engine built in Ada's lifetime?

properties.answer: No. Plans and trial pieces existed, but the complete Engine was not built.
properties.hint: Separate a design from a finished machine.
properties.prompt: Was the complete Analytical Engine built in Ada's lifetime?

anchor_records.display_name: How was the Engine's store like computer memory today?

properties.answer: Both ideas keep values available for later operations, but Babbage's mechanical store and modern electronic memory use radically different physical technology.
properties.hint: Use an analogy without claiming identical hardware.
properties.prompt: How was the Engine's store like computer memory today?

anchor_records.display_name: How were punched cards like programs today?

properties.answer: The cards represented instructions and data for the planned Engine. Modern programs are encoded electronically, but both organize operations into controllable sequences.
properties.prompt: How were punched cards like programs today?

anchor_records.display_name: How was studying mathematics different for Ada than for kids today?

properties.answer: Ada needed unusual private access to advanced study at a time when women faced strong educational barriers. Many more children can study mathematics today, although access is still not equal everywhere.
properties.hint: Do not turn progress into a claim that every barrier disappeared.
properties.prompt: How was studying mathematics different for Ada than for kids today?

anchor_records.display_name: Ada ran her program on a completed Analytical Engine.

properties.correction: The complete Engine was never built; Note G was a published procedure designed for it.
properties.kid_response: Ada wrote a plan for what the machine should do, but there was no finished Engine to run it.
properties.misconception: Ada ran her program on a completed Analytical Engine.

anchor_records.display_name: Ada designed and physically built Babbage's engine by herself.

properties.correction: Babbage designed the engines; Lovelace interpreted, explained, extended, and publicized their possibilities.
properties.kid_response: Babbage designed the machine, while Ada made major contributions through mathematics, explanation, and published notes.
properties.misconception: Ada designed and physically built Babbage's engine by herself.

anchor_records.display_name: Every historian agrees without qualification that Ada was the first programmer.

properties.correction: Note G is often called the first published program, but simplified first-person labels remain historically debated.
properties.kid_response: The label is useful, but careful history also explains the debate and the evidence behind it.
properties.misconception: Every historian agrees without qualification that Ada was the first programmer.

anchor_records.display_name: Lovelace claimed the Engine could originate ideas and think independently.

properties.correction: Her Notes emphasized following operations and relationships supplied to the Engine, while exploring how broadly symbols might be represented.
properties.kid_response: Ada imagined broad uses, but she did not describe the Engine as inventing its own instructions.
properties.misconception: Lovelace claimed the Engine could originate ideas and think independently.

anchor_records.display_name: A first-person Time Room scene is a recovered diary entry or exact quotation.

properties.correction: Runtime scenes are labeled reconstructions grounded by separate historical claims.
properties.kid_response: The scene helps us imagine the setting; the Fact lens tells us what the sources actually support.
properties.misconception: A first-person Time Room scene is a recovered diary entry or exact quotation.

anchor_records.display_name: Ada Lovelace alpha runtime pack

properties.guardrails: Use only included claims for factual assertions; label every scene as reconstruction; preserve qualified certainty; cite graph and source keys; never require Vellis or a model at runtime.
